added.claim_text: In the late 1950s and early 1960s, the United States was engaged in the Cold War, a geopolitical rivalry with the Soviet Union.
added.subject: the United States

qualifiers.spatial: the United States, the Soviet Union

added.claim_text: On October 4, 1957, the Soviet Union launched Sputnik 1, the first artificial satellite.
added.subject: the Soviet Union

added.claim_text: This surprise success fired fears and imaginations around the world.
added.subject: This surprise success

added.claim_text: It demonstrated that the Soviet Union had the capability to deliver nuclear weapons over intercontinental distances, and challenged American claims of military, economic and technological superiority.
added.subject: It

added.claim_text: President Dwight D. Eisenhower responded to the Sputnik challenge by creating the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), and initiating Project Mercury, which aimed to launch a man into Earth orbit.
added.subject: President Dwight D. Eisenhower

added.claim_text: But on April 12, 1961, Soviet cosmonaut Yuri Gagarin became the first person in space, and the first to orbit the Earth.
added.subject: Soviet cosmonaut Yuri Gagarin

added.claim_text: It was another body blow to American pride.
added.subject: It

added.claim_text: Nearly a month later, on May 5, 1961, Alan Shepard became the first American in space, completing a 15-minute suborbital journey.
added.subject: Alan Shepard

added.claim_text: After being recovered from the Atlantic Ocean, he received a congratulatory telephone call from Eisenhower's successor, John F. Kennedy.
added.subject: he

added.claim_text: Kennedy believed that it was in the national interest of the United States to be superior to other nations, and that the perception of American power was at least as important as the actuality.
added.subject: Kennedy

added.claim_text: It was therefore intolerable that the Soviet Union was more advanced in the field of space exploration.
added.subject: It

added.claim_text: He was determined that the United States should compete, and sought a challenge that maximized its chances of winning.
added.subject: He

qualifiers.conditional: should compete, and sought a challenge that maximized its chances of winning.

added.claim_text: Since the Soviet Union had better booster rockets, he required a challenge that was beyond the capacity of the existing generation of rocketry, one where the US and Soviet Union would be starting from a position of equality.
added.subject: he

qualifiers.spatial: the Soviet Union, US, Soviet Union

added.claim_text: Something spectacular, even if it could not be justified on military, economic or scientific grounds.

qualifiers.conditional: if it could not be justified on military, economic or scientific grounds.

added.claim_text: After consulting with his experts and advisors, he chose such a project.
added.subject: he

added.claim_text: On May 25, 1961, he addressed the United States Congress on "Urgent National Needs" and declared:
added.subject: he

added.claim_text: The initial crew assignment of Commander Neil Armstrong, Command Module Pilot (CMP) Jim Lovell, and Lunar Module Pilot (LMP) Buzz Aldrin on the backup crew for Apollo 9 was officially announced on November 20, 1967.
added.subject: The initial crew assignment

added.claim_text: Lovell and Aldrin had previously flown together as the crew of Gemini 12.
added.subject: Lovell

added.claim_text: Due to design and manufacturing delays in the LM, Apollo 8 and Apollo 9 swapped prime and backup crews, and Armstrong's crew became the backup for Apollo 8.
added.subject: design and manufacturing delays

added.claim_text: Based on the normal crew rotation scheme, Armstrong was then expected to command Apollo 11.
added.subject: Armstrong

added.claim_text: There would be one change.
Michael Collins, the CMP on the Apollo 8 crew, began experiencing trouble with his legs.
added.subject: Michael Collins

added.claim_text: Doctors diagnosed the problem as a bony growth between his fifth and sixth vertebrae, requiring surgery.
added.subject: Doctors

added.claim_text: Lovell took his place on the Apollo 8 crew, and when Collins recovered he joined Armstrong's crew as CMP.
added.subject: Lovell

added.claim_text: In the meantime, Fred Haise filled in as backup LMP, and Aldrin as backup CMP for Apollo 8.
added.subject: Fred Haise

added.claim_text: The next was STS-26 in 1988.
added.subject: next

added.claim_text: The Apollo 11 prime crew had none of the close cheerful camaraderie that characterized that of Apollo 12.
added.subject: The Apollo 11 prime crew

added.claim_text: Instead they forged an amiable working relationship.
added.subject: they

added.claim_text: Armstrong in particular was notoriously aloof, but Collins, who considered himself a loner, confessed to rebuffing Aldrin's attempts to create a more personal relationship.
added.subject: Armstrong

added.claim_text: Collins recalled a lone unfortunate incident in which Armstrong crashed the lunar module in a simulation, leaving both Armstrong and Aldrin indignant and embarrassed.
added.subject: Collins

added.claim_text: A heated discussion followed that evening, fueled by Aldrin's fondness for Scotch, but the next morning everything was back to normal and neither was perturbed.
added.subject: A heated discussion

added.claim_text: The backup crew consisted of Lovell as Commander, William Anders as CMP, and Haise as LMP.
added.subject: The backup crew

added.claim_text: Anders had flown with Lovell on Apollo 8.
added.subject: Anders

added.claim_text: In early 1969, he accepted a job with the National Aeronautics and Space Council effective August 1969, and announced that he would retire as an astronaut at that time.
added.subject: he

added.claim_text: Ken Mattingly was moved from the support crew into parallel training with Anders as backup CMP in case Apollo 11 was delayed past its intended July launch date, at which point Anders would be unavailable.
added.subject: Ken Mattingly

added.claim_text: Lovell, Haise, and Mattingly were later assigned as the prime crew of Apollo 13.
During Projects Mercury and Gemini, each mission had a prime and a backup crew.
added.subject: each mission

added.claim_text: For Apollo, a third crew of astronauts was added, known as the support crew.
added.subject: a third crew

added.claim_text: The support crew maintained the flight plan, checklists and mission ground rules, and ensured that the prime and backup crews were apprised of changes.
added.subject: The support crew

added.claim_text: They developed procedures, especially those for emergency situations, so these were ready for when the prime and backup crews came to train in the simulators, allowing them to concentrate on practicing and mastering them.
added.subject: these

added.claim_text: For Apollo 11, the support crew consisted of Ken Mattingly, Ronald Evans and Bill Pogue.
added.subject: the support crew

The capsule communicator (CAPCOM) was an astronaut at the Mission Control Center in Houston, Texas, who was the only person who communicated directly with the flight crew.
added.subject: The capsule communicator

added.claim_text: For Apollo 11, the CAPCOMs were: Charles Duke, Ronald Evans, Bruce McCandless II, James Lovell, William Anders, Ken Mattingly, Fred Haise, Don L. Lind, Owen K. Garriott and Harrison Schmitt.
added.subject: the CAPCOMs

added.claim_text: The four shift flight directors for this mission were:
added.subject: The four shift flight directors

added.claim_text:
*Clifford E. Charlesworth (Green team), launch and extravehicular activity (EVA)
*Gerald D. Griffin (Gold team)
*Gene Kranz (White team), lunar landing
*Glynn Lunney (Black team), lunar ascent
The Apollo 11 mission emblem was designed by Collins, who wanted a symbol for "peaceful lunar landing by the United States".
added.subject: The Apollo 11 mission emblem

added.claim_text: At Lovell's suggestion, he chose the bald eagle, the national bird of the United States, as the symbol.
added.subject: he

added.claim_text: Tom Wilson, a simulator instructor, suggested that they put an olive branch in its beak to represent their peaceful mission.
added.subject: Tom Wilson

added.claim_text: Collins added a lunar background with the Earth in the distance.
added.subject: Collins

added.claim_text: The sunlight in the image was coming from the wrong direction; the shadow should have been in the lower part of the Earth instead of the left.
added.subject: the shadow

qualifiers.conditional: should have been in the lower part of the Earth instead of the left.

added.claim_text: An illustrator at the MSC did the artwork, which was then sent off to NASA officials for approval.
added.subject: An illustrator

added.claim_text: The design was rejected.
added.subject: The design

added.claim_text: Bob Gilruth, the director of the MSC felt that the talons of the eagle looked "too warlike".
added.subject: Bob Gilruth

added.claim_text: After some discussion, the olive branch was moved to the talons.
added.subject: the olive branch

added.claim_text: When the Eisenhower dollar coin was released in 1971, the patch design provided the eagle for its reverse side.
added.subject: the patch design

added.claim_text: The name Snowcone was used for the CM and Haystack was used for the LM in both internal and external communications during early mission planning.
added.subject: The name

added.claim_text: The LM was named Eagle after the motif which was featured prominently on the mission insignia.
added.subject: The LM

added.claim_text: At Scheer's suggestion, the CM was named Columbia after Columbiad, the giant cannon that launched a spacecraft (also from Florida) in Jules Verne's 1865 novel From the Earth to the Moon.
added.subject: the CM

added.claim_text: In Collins' 1976 book, he said Columbia was in reference to Christopher Columbus.
added.subject: he

The astronaut had personal preference kits (PPKs), small bags containing personal items of significance that they wanted to take with them on the mission.
added.subject: The astronaut

added.claim_text: NASA's Apollo Site Selection Board announced five potential landing sites on February 8, 1968.
added.subject: NASA's Apollo Site Selection Board

added.claim_text: These were the result of two years' worth of studies based on high-resolution photography of the lunar surface by the five unmanned probes of the Lunar Orbiter program and information about surface conditions provided by the Surveyor program.
added.subject: These

added.claim_text: The best Earth-bound telescopes could not resolve features with the resolution Project Apollo required.
added.subject: The best Earth-bound telescopes

added.claim_text: The landing site had to be close to the lunar equator to minimize the amount of propellant required; clear of obstacles to minimize maneuvering, and flat to simplify the task of the landing radar.
added.subject: The landing site

added.claim_text: Scientific value was not a consideration.
added.subject: Scientific value

added.claim_text: Areas that appeared promising on photographs taken on Earth were often found to be totally unacceptable.
added.subject: Areas

added.claim_text: The original requirement that the site be free of craters had to be relaxed, as no such site was found.
added.subject: The original requirement

added.claim_text: Five sites were considered: Sites 1 and 2 were in the Sea of Tranquility (Mare Tranquilitatis); Site 3 was in the Central Bay (Sinus Medii); and Sites 4 and 5 were in the Ocean of Storms (Oceanus Procellarum).
added.subject: Site

qualifiers.spatial: the Sea of Tranquility, the Central Bay, the Ocean of Storms

added.claim_text:
The final site selection was based on seven criteria:
*
added.subject: The final site selection
one

qualifiers.conditional:
should a problem arise on the way to the Moon;
* with good visibility during the landing approach, meaning that the Sun

added.claim_text: The requirement for the Sun angle was particularly restrictive, limiting the launch date to one day per month.
added.subject: The requirement

added.claim_text: A landing just after dawn was chosen to limit the temperature extremes the astronauts would experience.
added.subject: A landing

added.claim_text: The Apollo Site Selection Board selected Site 2, with Sites 3 and 5 as backups in the event of the launch being delayed.
added.subject: The Apollo Site Selection Board

added.claim_text: In May 1969, Apollo 10's lunar module flew to within of Site 2, and reported that it was acceptable.
added.subject: Apollo 10's lunar module

added.claim_text: During the first press conference after the Apollo 11 crew was announced, the first question a reporter asked was, "Which one of you gentlemen will be the first man to step onto the lunar surface?"
added.subject: the first question

added.claim_text: Slayton told the reporter that it had not been decided, and Armstrong added that it was "not based on individual desire".
added.subject: Slayton

added.claim_text: One of the first versions of the egress checklist had the lunar module pilot exit the spacecraft before the command module pilot.
added.subject: One

added.claim_text: In prior missions, the commander never performed the spacewalk.
added.subject: the commander

added.claim_text: Reporters wrote in early 1969 that Aldrin would be the first to walk on the Moon, and Associate Administrator George Mueller told reporters he would be the first as well.
added.subject: Reporters

added.claim_text: Aldrin heard that Armstrong would be the first to step on the Moon because Armstrong was a civilian, which made Aldrin livid.
added.subject: Aldrin

added.claim_text: Aldrin attempted to persuade other lunar module pilots he should be first, but they responded cynically about what they perceived as a lobbying campaign.
added.subject: Aldrin

qualifiers.conditional: should be first, but they responded cynically about what they perceived as a lobbying campaign.

added.claim_text: Attempting to stem interdepartmental conflict, Slayton told Aldrin that Armstrong would be first since he was the commander.
added.subject: Slayton

added.claim_text: The decision was announced in a press conference on April 14, 1969.
added.subject: The decision

added.claim_text: For decades, Aldrin believed the final decision was largely driven by the lunar module's hatch location.
added.subject: Aldrin

added.claim_text: Because the astronauts had their spacesuits on and the spacecraft was so small, maneuvering to exit the spacecraft was difficult.
added.subject: the astronauts

added.claim_text: The crew tried a simulation in which Aldrin left the spacecraft first, but he damaged the simulator while attempting to egress.
added.subject: The crew

added.claim_text: While this was enough for mission planners to make their decision, Aldrin and Armstrong were left in the dark on the decision until late spring.
added.subject: Aldrin

added.claim_text: Slayton told Armstrong the plan was to have him leave the spacecraft first, if he agreed.
added.subject: Slayton

added.claim_text:
Armstrong said, "Yes, that’s the way to do it."

Kraft revealed in his 2001 autobiography that a meeting occurred between Gilruth, Slayton, Low, and himself to make sure Aldrin would not be the first to walk on the Moon.
added.subject: Armstrong

added.claim_text: They made the decision to change the flight plan so the commander was the first to egress from the spacecraft.
added.subject: They

The ascent stage of lunar module LM-5 arrived at the Kennedy Space Center on January 8, 1969, followed by the descent stage four days later, and Command and Service Module CM-107 on January 23.
added.subject: The ascent stage

added.claim_text: There were several differences between LM-5 and Apollo 10's LM-4; LM-5 had a VHF radio antenna to facilitate communication with the astronauts during their EVA on the lunar surface; a lighter ascent engine; more thermal protection on the landing gear; and a package of scientific experiments known as the Early Apollo Scientific Experiments Package (EASEP).
added.subject: LM-5

added.claim_text: The only change in the configuration of the command module was the removal of some insulation from the forward hatch.
added.subject: The only change

An estimated one million spectators watched the launch of Apollo 11 from the highways and beaches in the vicinity of the launch site.
added.subject: An estimated one million spectators

added.claim_text: Dignitaries included the Chief of Staff of the United States Army, General William Westmoreland, four cabinet members, 19 state governors, 40 mayors, 60 ambassadors and 200 congressmen.
added.subject: Dignitaries

added.claim_text: Vice President Spiro Agnew viewed the launch with the former president, Lyndon B. Johnson and his wife Lady Bird Johnson.
added.subject: Vice President Spiro Agnew

added.claim_text: Around 3,500 media representatives were present.
added.subject: Around 3,500 media representatives

added.claim_text: About two-thirds were from the United States; the rest came from 55 other countries.
added.subject: the rest

added.claim_text: The launch was televised live in 33 countries, with an estimated 25 million viewers in the United States alone.
added.subject: The launch

added.claim_text: Millions more around the world listened to radio broadcasts.
added.subject: Millions

added.claim_text: President Richard Nixon viewed the launch from his office in the White House with his NASA liaison officer, Apollo astronaut Frank Borman.
added.subject: President Richard Nixon

added.claim_text: At 12:52:00 UTC on July 20, Aldrin and Armstrong entered Eagle, and began the final preparations for lunar descent.
added.subject: Aldrin

added.claim_text: At 17:44:00 Eagle separated from Columbia.
added.subject: Eagle

added.claim_text: Armstrong exclaimed: "The Eagle has wings!"
added.subject: The Eagle

added.claim_text: Eagle was traveling too fast.
added.subject: Eagle

added.claim_text: The problem could have been mascons—concentrations of high mass that could have altered the trajectory.
added.subject: The problem

added.claim_text: Flight Director Gene Kranz speculated that it could have resulted from extra air pressure in the docking tunnel.
added.subject: Flight Director Gene Kranz

added.claim_text: Or it could have been the result of Eagles pirouette maneuver.
added.subject: it

added.claim_text: Five minutes into the descent burn, and above the surface of the Moon, the LM guidance computer (LGC) distracted the crew with the first of several unexpected 1201 and 1202 program alarms.
added.subject: the LM guidance computer

Margaret Hamilton, the Director of Apollo Flight Computer Programming at the MIT Charles Stark Draper Laboratory later recalled:

 photographed from Columbia]]
added.subject: Margaret Hamilton

added.claim_text: Armstrong considered landing short of the boulder field so they could collect geological samples from it, but could not since their horizontal velocity was too fast.
added.subject: Armstrong

added.claim_text: Now above the surface, Armstrong knew their propellant supply was dwindling and was determined to land at the first possible landing site.
added.subject: Armstrong

added.claim_text: Armstrong found a clear patch of ground and maneuvered the spacecraft towards it.
added.subject: Armstrong

added.claim_text: As he got closer, now above the surface, he discovered his new landing site had a crater in it.
added.subject: he

added.claim_text: He cleared the crater and found another patch of level ground.
added.subject: He

added.claim_text: They were now from the surface, with only 90 seconds of propellant remaining.
added.subject: They

added.claim_text: Lunar dust kicked up by the LM's engines began to obfuscate his ability to determine the spacecraft's motion.
added.subject: Lunar dust

added.claim_text: Some large rocks jutted out of the dust cloud, and Armstrong focused on them during his descent so he could determine the spacecraft's speed.
added.subject: Some large rocks

added.claim_text: Armstrong was supposed to immediately shutdown the engines, as the engineers suspected the pressure caused by the engines own exhaust reflecting off the lunar surface could make them explode, but forgot.
added.subject: Armstrong

added.claim_text: Aldrin continued: "Mode control – both auto.
added.subject: Aldrin

added.claim_text:
"

ACA was the attitude control assembly, the LM's control stick.
added.subject: ACA

added.claim_text: Output went to the LGC to command the reaction control system (RCS) jets to fire.
added.subject: Output

added.claim_text: "Out of Detent" meant that the stick had moved away from its centered position; it was spring-centered like the turn indicator in a car.
added.subject: it

added.claim_text: LGC address 413 contained the variable that indicated that the LM had landed.
added.subject: LGC address

added.claim_text: Eagle landed at 20:17:40 UTC on Sunday July 20 with about 25 seconds of fuel left.
added.subject: Eagle

added.claim_text: Preparations for the EVA began at 23:43.
added.subject: Preparations

added.claim_text: These took longer than expected; three and a half hours instead of two.
added.subject: These

added.claim_text: Some of the highest heart rates recorded from Apollo astronauts occurred during LM egress and ingress.
added.subject: Some

added.claim_text: At 02:51 Armstrong began his descent to the lunar surface.
added.subject: Armstrong

added.claim_text: The remote control unit controls on his chest kept him from seeing his feet.
added.subject: controls

added.claim_text: Climbing down the nine-rung ladder, Armstrong pulled a D-ring to deploy the modular equipment stowage assembly (MESA) folded against Eagle side and activate the TV camera.
added.subject: Armstrong

added.claim_text: This proved to be an inefficient tool, and later missions preferred to carry equipment and samples up to the LM by hand.
added.subject: This

added.claim_text: Nixon's speech writer William Safire had prepared In Event of Moon Disaster for the President to read on television in the event the Apollo 11 astronauts were stranded on the Moon.
added.subject: Nixon's speech writer

added.claim_text: During his day flying solo around the Moon, Collins never felt lonely.
added.subject: Collins

added.claim_text: Although it has been said "not since Adam has any human known such solitude", Collins felt very much a part of the mission.
added.subject: Collins

added.claim_text: In his autobiography he wrote: "this venture has been structured for three men, and I consider my third to be as necessary as either of the other two".
added.subject: he

added.claim_text: In the 48 minutes of each orbit when he was out of radio contact with the Earth while Columbia passed round the far side of the Moon, the feeling he reported was not fear or loneliness, but rather "awareness, anticipation, satisfaction, confidence, almost exultation".
added.subject: the feeling

added.claim_text: One of Collins' first tasks was to identify the lunar module on the ground.
added.subject: One

added.claim_text: To give Collins an idea where to look, Mission Control radioed that they believed the lunar module landed about four miles off target.
added.subject: Mission Control

added.claim_text: Each time he passed over the suspected lunar landing site, he tried in vain to find the module.
added.subject: he

added.claim_text: On his first orbits on the back side of the Moon, Collins performed maintenance activities such as dumping excess water produced by the fuel cells and preparing the cabin for Armstrong and Aldrin to return.
added.subject: Collins

added.claim_text: Just before he reached the dark side on the third orbit, Mission Control informed Collins that there was a problem with the temperature of the coolant.
added.subject: Mission Control

added.claim_text: If it became too cold, parts of Columbia might freeze.
added.subject: parts

qualifiers.conditional: If it became too cold, parts of Columbia might freeze.

added.claim_text: Mission Control advised him to assume manual control and implement Environmental Control System Malfunction Procedure 17.
added.subject: Mission Control

added.claim_text: Instead, Collins flicked the switch on the offending system from automatic to manual and back to automatic again, and carried on with normal housekeeping chores, while keeping an eye on the temperature.
added.subject: Collins

added.claim_text: When Columbia came back around to the near side of the Moon again, he was able to report that the problem had been resolved.
added.subject: he

added.claim_text: After Aldrin and Armstrong completed their EVA, Collins slept so he could be rested for the rendezvous.
added.subject: Collins

added.claim_text: While the flight plan called for Eagle to meet up with Columbia, Collins was prepared for certain contingencies in which he would fly Columbia down to meet Eagle.
added.subject: Collins

added.claim_text: Eagle rendezvoused with Columbia at 21:24 UTC on July 21, and the two docked at 21:35.
added.subject: Eagle

added.claim_text: Eagles ascent stage was jettisoned into lunar orbit at 23:41.
added.subject: Eagles ascent stage

added.claim_text: Just before the Apollo 12 flight, it was noted that Eagle was still likely to be orbiting the Moon.
added.subject: it

added.claim_text: Later NASA reports mentioned that Eagle orbit had decayed, resulting in it impacting in an "uncertain location" on the lunar surface.
added.subject: reports

added.claim_text: The aircraft carrier , under the command of Captain Carl J. Seiberlich, was selected as the primary recovery ship (PRS) for Apollo 11 on June 5, replacing its sister ship, the LPH , which had recovered Apollo 10 on May 26.
added.subject: The aircraft carrier

added.claim_text: Hornet was then at her home port of Long Beach, California.
added.subject: Hornet

added.claim_text: On reaching Pearl Harbor on July 5, Hornet embarked the Sikorsky SH-3 Sea King helicopters of HS-4, a unit which specialized in recovery of Apollo spacecraft, specialized divers of UDT Detachment Apollo, a 35-man NASA recovery team, and about 120 media representatives.
added.subject: Hornet
added.object: the Sikorsky SH-3 Sea King helicopters

added.claim_text: To make room, most of Hornets air wing was left behind in Long Beach.
added.subject: most

added.claim_text: Special recovery equipment was also loaded, including a boilerplate command module used for training.
added.subject: Special recovery equipment

added.claim_text: On July 12, with Apollo 11 still on the launch pad, Hornet departed Pearl Harbor for the recovery area in the central Pacific, in the vicinity of .
added.subject: Hornet

added.claim_text: On August 13, the three astronauts rode in parades in their honor in New York, Chicago, and Los Angeles.
added.subject: the three astronauts

added.claim_text: The main repository for the Apollo Moon rocks is the Lunar Sample Laboratory Facility at the Lyndon B. Johnson Space Center in Houston, Texas.
added.subject: The main repository

qualifiers.spatial: the Lyndon B. Johnson Space Center, Houston, Texas

added.claim_text: For safekeeping, there is also a smaller collection stored at White Sands Test Facility near Las Cruces, New Mexico.
Most of the rocks are stored in nitrogen to keep them free of moisture.
added.subject: Most

added.claim_text: Over 100 research laboratories around the world conduct studies of the samples, and approximately 500 samples are prepared and sent to investigators every year.
added.subject: Over 100 research laboratories

added.claim_text:
* – 200+ pages
* – 230 pages

removed.claim_text: In early 1969, Anders accepted a job with the National Space Council effective August 1969 and announced that he would retire as an astronaut on that date.
removed.subject: Anders

removed.claim_text: At that point Ken Mattingly was moved from the support crew into parallel training with Anders as backup Command Module Pilot in case Apollo 11 was delayed past its intended July launch (at which point Anders would be unavailable if needed) and would later join Lovell's crew and ultimately be assigned as the original Apollo 13 CMP.
removed.subject: Ken Mattingly

qualifiers.conditional: if needed) and would later join Lovell's crew and ultimately be assigned as the original Apollo 13 CMP.

During early mission planning, the names Snowcone and Haystack were used and put in the news release, but the crew later decided to change them.
removed.subject: the names

removed.claim_text: The Command Module was named Columbia after the Columbiad, the giant cannon shell "spacecraft" fired by a giant cannon (also from Florida) in Jules Verne's 1865 novel From the Earth to the Moon.
removed.subject: The Command Module

removed.claim_text:
]]
The Apollo 11 mission insignia was designed by Collins, who wanted a symbol for "peaceful lunar landing by the United States".
removed.subject: The Apollo 11 mission insignia

removed.claim_text: He chose an eagle as the symbol, put an olive branch in its beak, and drew a lunar background with the Earth in the distance.
removed.subject: He

removed.claim_text: NASA officials said the talons of the eagle looked too "warlike" and after some discussion, the olive branch was moved to the claws.
removed.subject: NASA officials

removed.claim_text: Neil Armstrong's personal preference kit carried a piece of wood from the Wright brothers' 1903 airplane's left propeller and a piece of fabric from its wing, along with a diamond-studded astronaut pin originally given to Deke Slayton by the widows of the Apollo 1 crew.
removed.subject: Neil Armstrong's personal preference kit

removed.claim_text: This pin had been intended to be flown on Apollo 1 and given to Slayton after the mission but following the disastrous launch pad fire and subsequent funerals, the widows gave the pin to Slayton and Armstrong took it on Apollo 11.
removed.subject: the widows

removed.claim_text: In addition to many people crowding highways and beaches near the launch site, millions watched the event on television, with NASA Chief of Public Information Jack King providing commentary.
removed.subject: millions

removed.claim_text: President Richard M. Nixon viewed the proceedings from the Oval Office of the White House.
removed.subject: President Richard M. Nixon

removed.claim_text: A Saturn V launched Apollo 11 from Launch Pad 39A, part of the Launch Complex 39 site at the Kennedy Space Center on July 16, 1969, at 13:32:00 UTC (9:32:00 a.m. EDT local time).
removed.subject: A Saturn V

removed.claim_text: After one and a half orbits, the S-IVB third-stage engine pushed the spacecraft onto its trajectory toward the Moon with the trans-lunar injection (TLI) burn at 16:22:13 UTC.
removed.subject: the S-IVB third-stage engine

removed.claim_text: About 30 minutes later, the transposition, docking, and extraction maneuver was performed: this involved separating the Apollo Command/Service Module (CSM) from the spent rocket stage, turning around, and docking with the Lunar Module still attached to the stage.
removed.subject: the transposition

removed.claim_text: After the Lunar Module was extracted, the combined spacecraft headed for the Moon, while the rocket stage flew on a trajectory past the Moon and into orbit around the Sun.
removed.subject: the Lunar Module

removed.claim_text: On July 19 at 17:21:50 UTC, Apollo 11 passed behind the Moon and fired its service propulsion engine to enter lunar orbit.
removed.subject: Apollo

removed.claim_text: In the thirty orbits that followed, the crew saw passing views of their landing site in the southern Sea of Tranquility (Mare Tranquillitatis) about southwest of the crater Sabine D (0.67408N, 23.47297E).
removed.subject: the crew

removed.claim_text: The landing site was selected in part because it had been characterized as relatively flat and smooth by the automated Ranger 8 and Surveyor 5 landers along with the Lunar Orbiter mapping spacecraft and unlikely to present major landing or extravehicular activity (EVA) challenges.
removed.subject: The landing site

On July 20, 1969, the Lunar Module Eagle separated from the Command Module Columbia.
removed.subject: the Lunar Module Eagle

removed.claim_text: Five minutes into the descent burn, and above the surface of the Moon, the LM navigation and guidance computer distracted the crew with the first of several unexpected "1202" and "1201" program alarms.
removed.subject: the LM navigation and guidance computer

Armstrong took semi-automatic control

Apollo 11 landed with less fuel than other missions, and the astronauts encountered a premature low fuel warning.
removed.subject: Armstrong

removed.claim_text: This was later found to be the result of greater propellant 'slosh' than expected, uncovering a fuel sensor.
removed.subject: This

removed.claim_text: On subsequent missions, extra anti-slosh baffles were added to the tanks to prevent this.
removed.subject: extra anti-slosh baffles

The astronauts planned placement of the Early Apollo Scientific Experiment Package (EASEP)
removed.subject: The astronauts

removed.claim_text: Armstrong reminded Aldrin of a bag of memorial items in his suit pocket sleeve, and Aldrin tossed the bag down; Armstrong then jumped to the ladder's third rung and climbed into the LM.
removed.subject: Armstrong

removed.claim_text: After transferring to LM life support, the explorers lightened the ascent stage for return to lunar orbit by tossing out their PLSS backpacks, lunar overshoes, one Hasselblad camera, and other equipment.
removed.subject: the explorers

removed.claim_text: President Nixon's speech writer William Safire had prepared In Event of Moon Disaster for the President to read on television in the event the Apollo 11 astronauts were stranded on the Moon.
removed.subject: President Nixon's speech writer

removed.claim_text: The contingency plan originated in a memo from Safire to Nixon's White House Chief of Staff H. R. Haldeman, in which Safire suggested a protocol the administration might follow in reaction to such a disaster.
removed.subject: The contingency plan

removed.claim_text: On July 24, the astronauts returned home aboard the Command Module Columbia just before dawn local time (16:51 UTC) at , in the Pacific Ocean east of Wake Island, south of Johnston Atoll, and from the recovery ship, .
removed.subject: the astronauts

removed.claim_text: The American Samoa-flag which was brought to the moon by Apollo 11 is on display at the Jean P. Haydon Museum in Pago Pago, the territorial capital of American Samoa.
removed.subject: REF

removed.claim_text: At 16:44 UTC the drogue parachutes had been deployed and seven minutes later the Command Module struck the water forcefully.
During splashdown, the Command Module landed upside down but was righted within 10 minutes by flotation bags triggered by the astronauts.
removed.subject: the Command Module

removed.claim_text: Our checklist is complete.
removed.subject: Our checklist

removed.claim_text: Awaiting swimmers", was Armstrong's last official transmission from Columbia.
A diver from the Navy helicopter hovering above attached a sea anchor to the Command Module to prevent it from drifting.
removed.subject: A diver

removed.claim_text: Additional divers attached flotation collars to stabilize the module and position rafts for astronaut extraction.
removed.subject: Additional divers

removed.claim_text: Though the chance of bringing back pathogens from the lunar surface was considered remote, it was considered a possibility and NASA took great precautions at the recovery site.
removed.subject: it

removed.claim_text: Divers provided the astronauts with Biological Isolation Garments (BIGs) which were worn until they reached isolation facilities on board the Hornet.
removed.subject: Divers

removed.claim_text: Additionally, astronauts were rubbed down with a sodium hypochlorite solution and the Command Module wiped with Betadine to remove any lunar dust that might be present.
removed.subject: astronauts

removed.claim_text: The raft containing decontamination materials was then intentionally sunk.
removed.subject: The raft

removed.claim_text:
A second Sea King helicopter - "Helicopter 66" - hoisted the astronauts aboard one by one, where a NASA flight surgeon gave each a brief physical check during the trip back to the Hornet.

 after returning to Earth, visited by Richard Nixon]]

After touchdown on the Hornet, the astronauts exited the helicopter, leaving the flight surgeon and three crewmen.
removed.subject: the astronauts

removed.claim_text: The helicopter was then lowered into hangar bay #2 where the astronauts walked the to the Mobile Quarantine Facility (MQF) where they would begin the earth-based portion of their 21 days of quarantine.
removed.subject: The helicopter

removed.claim_text: The Soviet Union had been competing with the US in landing a man on the Moon but had been hampered by repeated failures in development of a launcher comparable to the Saturn V.
removed.subject: The Soviet Union

removed.claim_text: Meanwhile, they tried to beat the US to return lunar material to the Earth by means of unmanned probes.
removed.subject: they

removed.claim_text: On July 13, three days before Apollo 11's launch, they launched Luna 15, which reached lunar orbit before Apollo 11.
removed.subject: they

removed.claim_text: During descent, a malfunction caused Luna 15 to crash in Mare Crisium about two hours before Armstrong and Aldrin took off from the Moon's surface to begin their voyage home.
removed.subject: a malfunction

removed.claim_text: The Jodrell Bank Observatory radio telescope in England was later discovered to have recorded transmissions from Luna 15 during its descent, and this was published in July 2009 on the 40th anniversary of Apollo 11.
removed.subject: The Jodrell Bank Observatory radio telescope

removed.claim_text: It was in the central Milestones of Flight exhibition hall in front of the Jefferson Drive entrance, sharing the main hall with other pioneering flight vehicles such as the Wright Flyer, the Spirit of St. Louis, the Bell X-1, the North American X-15, Mercury spacecraft Friendship 7, and Gemini 4.
removed.subject: It

removed.claim_text: The quarantine trailer, the flotation collar, and the righting spheres are displayed at the Smithsonian's Steven F. Udvar-Hazy Center annex near Washington Dulles International Airport in Chantilly, Virginia.
removed.subject: The quarantine trailer

qualifiers.spatial: Washington Dulles International Airport, Chantilly, Virginia

removed.claim_text: In 2009, the Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter (LRO) imaged the various Apollo landing sites on the surface of the Moon, for the first time with sufficient resolution to see the descent stages of the lunar modules, scientific instruments, and foot trails made by the astronauts.
removed.subject: the Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter

removed.claim_text: In March 2012 a team of specialists financed by Amazon founder Jeff Bezos located the F-1 engines that launched Apollo 11 into space.
removed.subject: a team

removed.claim_text: The engines were found below the Atlantic Ocean's surface through the use of advanced sonar scanning.
removed.subject: The engines
